name: David
classification_notes: David displays wide proportions, squarish counters and a true italic with distinct shape changes, characteristics consistent with humanist sans-serif design rather than geometric or grotesque conventions.
summary: A wide-proportioned sans-serif with squarish counters and a true italic, drawn from a 1969 lettering book by David Gates and refined into a six-weight family.
description: Originating from a sample alphabet published by David Gates in 1969, the design was adapted by Emilie Rigaud with wider proportions and more squarish counters before growing into a full family of six weights from Extralight to Heavy. Rather than a simple slanted oblique, the italic was developed as a true italic with distinct shape changes and deeper junctions. The family has found a consistent home in editorial and magazine work, including Papiers France Culture, Entorse and Girls like us.
foundry_blurb: A is for describe David as a font family rooted in a 1969 lettering-reference book by David Gates, reworked with wider proportions and squarish counters. The family spans six weights with true italics, and has been adopted by several magazines for its editorial character.

name: Tongari
classification_notes: Tongari features strong stroke contrast and sharp, pointed terminals in its Display variant, placing it firmly in the high-contrast serif tradition. The Text variant moderates these features for continuous reading, but the overall design language is defined by its sharp, calligraphically inflected structure.
summary: A high-contrast serif in two optical variants, named after a Japanese word for sharp-pointed, with seven weights and matching italics throughout.
description: Designed by Émilie Rigaud, Tongari spans a Text family with tempered contrast for continuous reading and a Display family that revives the original spirit of the drawings, with stronger contrast, a higher x-height and sharper terminals. The Display cut includes alternate forms such as a double-storey g with an open tail, making it well-suited to magazine headlines and exhibition graphics. The family has been used in practice for exhibitions including a J.R.R. Tolkien show at the Bibliothèque nationale de France.
foundry_blurb: Tongari is a type family of seven weights and seven italics, with its name taken from the Japanese word for sharp-pointed. The foundry draws a parallel to the seven samurai of Kurosawa's film. The design began with a high-contrast medium weight featuring sharp endings, which were softened for the Text family to suit continuous reading at small sizes. The Display version returns to the original drawings, adding stronger contrast, a higher x-height and alternate letterforms for use in big titles and exhibition design.

name: Tongari
classification_notes: The sharp, pointed forms suggested by the name and samurai reference point to a humanist sans design with distinctive terminals; the dual Text and Display optical sizes reinforce a well-considered type family structure.
summary: A 14-style type family by Émilie Rigaud, split across Text and Display optical sizes, with seven weights inspired by the seven samurai of Kurosawa.
description: Designed by Émilie Rigaud at French foundry A is for, Tongari takes its name from the Japanese word for 'sharp-pointed', a quality reflected in its letterforms. The family runs across two optical subfamilies, Tongari Text and Tongari Display, each offering seven weights from Light to Black with matching italics. The concept ties each weight to one of the seven samurai from Kurosawa's film, giving the family a coherent thematic identity alongside its typographic range.
foundry_blurb: A is for describe Tongari as a type family of 7 weights and their 7 italics. The name comes from the Japanese word for 'sharp-pointed', a quality likened to the swords of the seven samurai in Akira Kurosawa's film, in which warriors defend a village from thieves. Seven weights for seven samurai.

name: BTP
classification_notes: BTP is derived from the default AutoCAD stroke typeface, resulting in a strictly angular, single-stroke-inspired geometric construction with no curves. Its pictogram set and rotated italic variants place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A strictly angular typeface derived from AutoCAD's default stroke font, built with no curves and accompanied by a set of construction-themed pictograms.
description: Originating from a typographic experiment in issue #195 of the French magazine Étapes in 2011, BTP was developed by Jeremy Perrodeau and Guillaume Grall as a formal response to the lettering conventions of architectural drawing software. The family comes in Light and Normal weights, each with a rotated variant serving as an italic substitute, plus seven interpolated weights in an extended family. A set of around 30 pictograms, including brick walls and road patterns, can be used to create borders and decorative frames in the manner of traditional metal ornaments.
foundry_blurb: BTP stands for Building, Typing, Publishing. The design is freely inspired by the default typeface found in AutoCAD, the computer-assisted design software used by architects. After typographic experiments in Étapes magazine in 2011, Jeremy Perrodeau and Guillaume Grall developed the face into a full family featuring angular curves, rotated italics, pictograms, and two stylistic sets for light punctuation and tabular capitals.

name: Coline
classification_notes: Coline draws from 16th-century French printers, particularly Simon de Colines, producing a humanist serif with strong pen-drawn qualities. The three subfamilies progress from a conventional roman to a near-cursive upright, all informed by historical small-format book typography.
summary: A seven-style family designed for small-format books, structured in three upright variants that graduate from a classical roman to a near-handwritten form.
description: Rooted in the small-format printing of 16th-century French publisher Simon de Colines, the family is built around compact proportions, generous x-height and large counters suited to tight settings. Its three members, Premiere, Cursive and Extreme, are all upright typefaces distinguished by their instrokes, outstrokes and rhythm rather than slant, giving designers a range of contrast without a conventional italic. The Black weight of Coline Extreme pushes the sculpted mass approach furthest, with deliberate cuts and blistered curves borrowed from the sketch process.
foundry_blurb: Coline is described as a typeface family especially designed for small-format books such as pocket books, structured in three sets, Coline Premiere, Coline Cursive and Coline Extreme, growing from a sturdy pure roman to a nearly handwritten design. The name derives from Simon de Colines, one of the first French printers to publish cheap small-format books for students in the 16th century. The family is condensed to save space and features large counters for legibility at small sizes.
